attributes.stem: A 60-year-old woman is admitted to the hospital with a diagnosis of myocardial infarction. On the fifth day of hospitalisation, the patient experiences a sudden onset of shortness of breath. During the physical examination, a pan-systolic murmur is heard at the apex of the heart. The murmur does not increase in intensity with deep, held inspiration. Bibasilar inspiratory crackles are heard in both lung bases. No neck vein distension is observed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following complications has most likely occurred?
attributes.explanation:
The patient's clinical presentation of sudden onset of dyspnoea with the presence of bibasilar inspiratory crackles indicates pulmonary oedema. The presence of a pan-systolic murmur at the apex of the heart suggests mitral regurgitation. The most likely cause of this clinical presentation is posteromedial papillary muscle rupture. The posteromedial papillary muscle in the left ventricle is supplied by the right coronary artery. The rupture of this muscle results in acute mitral regurgitation leading to left-sided heart failure and pulmonary oedema. The murmur associated with mitral regurgitation is typically pan-systolic and is heard best at the apex of the heart. The intensity of the murmur does not increase with deep, held inspiration. Additionally, bibasilar inspiratory crackles are often heard in patients with pulmonary oedema due to fluid accumulation in the lungs.

A ventricular aneurysm is a bulge in the wall of the heart that can develop after myocardial infarction. These aneurysms are less likely to rupture because their walls are composed of scar tissue. However, if rupture does occur, it may lead to acute myocardial infarction and sudden death.

Rupture of the posterior wall of the heart is a rare complication that occurs most commonly after myocardial infarction. This type of rupture causes cardiac tamponade, a condition in which fluid accumulates in the pericardium (the sac that surrounds the heart), leading to compression of the heart and impaired cardiac function, which ultimately leads to sudden death. It is not associated with any murmurs.

Interventricular wall rupture is a rare complication that can occur after myocardial infarction. This type of rupture causes a systolic murmur with a left-to-right shunt, resulting in right-sided heart failure.

Rupture of the anterior wall of the heart is a rare but potentially fatal complication that occurs most commonly after myocardial infarction. This type of rupture causes cardiac tamponade, a condition in which fluid accumulates in the pericardium, leading to compression of the heart and impaired cardiac function. Unlike other types of ruptures, no murmurs are found in this type of rupture.
learningPoints: Acute myocardial infarction can lead to various complications, including papillary muscle rupture, which can cause acute mitral regurgitation and pulmonary oedema.
Papillary muscle rupture is a life-threatening complication of myocardial infarction that requires immediate recognition and intervention.
references: Burton LV, Beier K. Papillary Muscle Rupture. 2023 May 22. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan–. PMID: 29763151. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29763151/

Harari R, Bansal P, Yatskar L, Rubinstein D, Silbiger JJ. Papillary muscle rupture following acute myocardial infarction: Anatomic, echocardiographic, and surgical insights. Echocardiography. 2017 Nov;34(11):1702-1707. doi: 10.1111/echo.13739. Epub 2017 Oct 29. PMID: 29082549. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/29082549/

Labrada L, Patil A, Kumar J, Kolman S, Iftikhar O, Keane M, Minakata K, Van Decker W, Lakhter V, Whitman I. Papillary Muscle Rupture Complicating Acute Myocardial Infarction: A Tale of Teamwork. JACC Case Rep. 2020 Dec 16;2(15):2283-2288. doi: 10.1016/j.jaccas.2020.08.027. PMID: 34317156; PMCID: PMC8304571.
